speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Nathan Fefer, F-E-F-E-R. Today's date is June 30, 1995. This is tape number three. When you went back to where you were in hiding, how long had it been since you had left that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the other three men still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were there, sure. They thought maybe they will start beating me and I will tell the police. But I wouldn't send the police. My mother used to come to us, Easter time, there in the bunker, to bring the water, the Easter water from the church, and sprinkle on us to live through. And she used to say, just preach to Jesus. Then you will live through. And the boy, your brother, used to take them out in the fields a little bit, during the daytime. Said, nobody knows you. But if you were here, they would know you. Now they were in the fields for a couple hours. They came back. And she, the girl, used to take me in the nighttime, a little bit of work. Well, they thought, after the war, the parents came to me. And they thought I was going to marry her. But I wouldn't do it. She deserved, you know. She deserved, but I couldn't do this. I send them money all the time. Now she's dead. But I sent her money, packages always. But they took me, no chance to live, you know. They were good people, I'm telling you. With their whole heart, they want us to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] She got two guns for us. We thought in case something happened, the Germans will catch us. So we talk to each other, we kill each other, and the last bullet, you kill yourself. But it never happened. Two guns she got for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and the other men used to talk about together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you mean with the Polish people?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the four of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything that we went through. So after the liberation, we went out. So I was still-- the war was still not finished because the war was finished in '45, but this was before. So the Russians was there, and they check everybody with a passport. And I don't have any passport. I told them I'm a Jew. He said in Russian, Jew, no Jew, you have to have a passport. So they took me to the office with the Russian. So I told them, I told the officer. Let's take a look. I'm from here. I was hidden here until-- and I don't have any document.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he wants me to go with me and to show where was it. And already he talks to me in Russian, and the people, the Polish people around me. And I told them in Russian, you see the people here? They would know I'm there, I wouldn't be alive today. And I can't go with you, because later they'll killed the Polish people hiding me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, OK. He gave me a cigarette. He gave me food. He said, go. Just tell my name, and they wouldn't bother anybody. This was all right. I went there to Lublin. Came to Lublin, was already a lot of Jewish people there. There was already a committee, a Jewish committee, from the Joint and the HIAS there. So the two guys with me, they went in the police, the Polish police. And I didn't want to go to Polish police. I'd like to do something-- business, you know, to make a living. What I needed Polish police there. And that's it. I start little by little, little by little there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me exactly how you knew about being liberated, how that came about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure, because when I saw the Russian people who came in the village when I was hidden there. So I saw  the Russian people who talk in Russian. But before the war, the Russian didn't have these things like for the czar. So starting in the wartime, he did it to the soldiers-- the same thing like before the First War.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was afraid, maybe the Germans, they did this trick, because I can't believe it. And she comes to us, the woman. She said, were you crazy? There are Russian people there-- go out, go out. So two days I was still in that attic. I was afraid to go out. Later I went out. So I start talking with them. And when was this?

words.text: So I start talking with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '44. It was I think July or August, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other men who were with you-- were they as nervous about going out as you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. And they told me, you go, because you speak Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you tell them when you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them, come on. Let's go. Be free. Be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back during all of those times, what do you think stands out as the hardest pieces?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I thought, I will go home, which I knew, nobody will have you. So to go home, you have to have a passport to go through the whole thing. So I have to go to the office there from the Russian to take a passport. So I told them, I just came out from [INAUDIBLE], and I'd like to go to see home. Right away, when I talked to him, I saw right away something fishy. He told me, OK. You have to go home. But first you have to go in the army to fight the Germans. [INAUDIBLE]. Me, like a dead person. I just came out. He said, it's not our business. This, we have to do. And he took two soldiers, the Russian soldiers, and took me back there. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took you where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a place, you know, where the whole soldiers came together, the Russian soldiers. And at that time, they were fighting by Warsaw. The Polish, they lost thousands and thousands of people by Warsaw, because they didn't let the Russian people fight. They said, we will fight ourselves. It's Poland. So they lost thousands and thousands of people. They want me to go to fight. I didn't go. I run away at the right time. I didn't go. I didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I run away, I left him the passport. I had a passport. I told him, take a look. I came here voluntarily. And now you took me there. You lock me up. And the people there, if they know me, they will think I disappeared all of a sudden. I  lived through the war, and I'm right here. Take my passport, and tomorrow I'll come back to here. Tomorrow, I never saw that tomorrow. So I left the passport there. Then I needed a passport. I went into the Polish bureau, and I made a passport, Polish [NON-ENGLISH]. A new passport. With this passport, I went all over Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me the name you used again?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] I've got it there written down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the passport that you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's the passport that they gave me, so I got a passport. So already, you know, I started doing a little bit better. You have to live, you know. Because they got a home-- like an old-age home. You know, the old people, the survivors went there. And they got-- there was a kitchen. But I didn't like it here. There was people there, I never saw them. I liked to live for myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So three people, we took their house there from the ghetto, and we lived there. So I started doing a little bit of business to survive, to make a living. Who were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With three people and other people, you know. Survivors too, but they were survivors in the woods, you know. They lived through the woods. So they later started going there. And then [INAUDIBLE] with Poland again. They killed-- in that time when I was there, they killed maybe 30 people, Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know why? People when they run away to hiding, they left their things by the Polish labor. This was the worst thing to go back to tell them to give you back this thing. So what they did, they said, OK, come tomorrow. We'll give you back. So before you came to him, you killed him on the road. So I thought, it's no good. So then later, I went further, further, further, further, further. And that's when I met her. How did you meet your wife?

words.text: And that's when I met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her there, not knowing even there. So I went in there, and we started talking, talking. And we became friends. And we went together to Germany from there. It was not so easy to go to Germany, but we went. Another trouble, you know, because here was the Poland, and here was the Russian. You have to cross the border. And without a pass, you can't cross the border. And the Polish people, they wouldn't let you out too. They want to rob you, this and this. So there used to be Russian people with trucks and take people to the border. But you pay them a couple hundred dollars-- this I don't remember. So you cross the border. And some Russian people took the money, and they let you in this border too. But we did it and we passed the border. We went to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time of the war, before you were liberated, had you ever become sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I was never sick there. One time I was sick, because you can't be sick there. If you're sick, you go to the gas chamber. If you're sick, you have to work anyhow. But I never got a cold, working in the winter like this. Never got sick. But I was a good worker, you know. If you're a good worker, the Germans like you. And one time, we were unloading potatoes, you know, for the Germans. So there were wheelbarrows. You have to fill up the wheelbarrow with potatoes. So the guy-- I was inside to fill up the-- he used to go over this with a wheelbarrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Germans saw me not filling up full. So they called me up. Why you didn't fill up? So what I can say? I can say nothing, because when I would say, he told me not to fill up, if I get beaten up, I'll get beaten. So I made like this, like I don't know what he's talking to me. So he gave me 25 sticks on the back. This was the first time I was beaten there, on account this.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] And then the Germans-- in DP camp, three years there. I was sick. I was in a sanatorium one year on the lungs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you end up in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was the Jewish agency. Everybody who lived through Hitler they pushed to Germany. Let Germans support you, because you don't have any home. So they pushed everybody to Germany, and they make camps there, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a room, a half a room from this, four people together. After I got married already, there. So there from Germany, after three years, they took us to-- Did you get married in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. Yeah, married. I was just married. I have still the [NON-ENGLISH]. I had a problem later. Before you enter there, you have to go to the FBI. So a stupid guy telling me to show him the [NON-ENGLISH], the ketubah. It was written in Hebrew. It was written in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said it was in English. I thought, I can do it. He wouldn't let me go to the United States. It took me two weeks extra. Until I find in the American army a Jewish captain. He went to a rabbi, and he translated this to English. How long did it take-- did you learn of what had happened to your brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: The last you had heard of him, he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there. In '43, they took all 4,000 people to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it, but I was already hidden. See, because the Germans used to say to us-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. Prisoners of war, after the war, everybody will go home. Just the prisoners. So people trust them. They thought, yes. And secondly, there was nowhere to run away, so where do they run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, they tried to run away to the Polish partisan. 100 people went there just to figure out how to do it. So what did they do to them? They throw hand grenades into the barracks and killed them. So one guy leftover, he came and said, we can't go. Nowhere to go. When you were in hiding, did you have any awareness of what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure. They got papers every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the woman who was hiding you would bring you news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. She was just-- I need to go to church, go to church Sundays. Go to church. Go every Sunday. I thought, no, I wouldn't go. I'm afraid to go. They were good people, it's no excuse. They were all dead. It was just two who lived there. The boy was such a good boy. He died-- 50 years old he died. And the girl died already. I got pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever make it back to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's nothing to go there. I know they're not alive, so why do I go there? For the property? So they kill me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that no one had survive-- that no one in your family had survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, two girls from my-- they were alive. And they said, you know, they took them out. And they told me where they got killed. And I know where they're buried there too. She told me. But they were hiding, so they lived through the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these two girls were in hiding, but they knew of the fate of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I wouldn't go to the army, I would save a couple of sisters, because I knew the way to go. I would save a couple. But-- what would you do? My sisters were one by one beautiful. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any relatives who survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm just the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think it is that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Sometimes I think I should go with them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's not so easy. Six sisters. Beautiful sisters. And now this day, they hate us too. Anti-Semitism again. The Jew, the Jew, the Jew, the Jew, the Jew. There's an article in the Bob Dole, an article, he was anti-Semitic, from the top to the bottom, the whole article. A book-- he writes a book. So he wants to be president. Or Pat Buchanan-- he said, it's impossible. This gas couldn't gas the people. Well, they did it. They took young girls. They made prostitutes for them, for the German army, if they should-- if they would go on the front to Russia, they took them for prostitutes. They picked up, one by one.

words.text: this day, they hate us too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did this experience do for you in terms of your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I tell you the truth, if not my father, I wouldn't-- but see, my father was religious, and he was a good father. So I go to shul. I belong to synagogue. I belong to the brotherhood. I belong to B'nai Brith. I belong to the BYO. All over, I got-- every organization I belonged in. Jewish National Front, I have three awards, three pictures I got in the other room. But I did it for them. And then at 74, I sold off everything, and I went to Israel. So I retired at 63. I went to Israel. And I settled down there. I got still a passport, an Israeli citizen. But I went there not to make any business, just to retire to see Israel before I die. So we went volunteer work for the army, I and my wife. They took us every morning to the camp there. And we got dinner with the soldiers. And we worked all day. What we did it, they got the mask on the war, the soldiers, if you wear one time, from the scent, they spoiled. So they used to clean the masks. So we worked there a couple months like this there.

words.text: three pictures I got in the other room.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got sick, and I got an operation. So you couldn't do any more work. It was sitting in the house. So my daughter said, you know what, go to Florida for the winter and see how Florida is. So I came here, and I liked it. So I sold the house there, and I came back here. In 1980, I came back right here. You had originally come to the United States in what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: In '49. And you ended up in Connecticut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not just that, but in '51, I was already a farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: A farmer in Connecticut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you raise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chickens for eggs. And I got a couple cows. You know, I was a millionaire. And then I start, little by little, building up, building up. When I sold out, I have already 30,000 chicks.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I got two daughters. Marilyn and Fay. This is my beautiful daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message on this tape that you would like to leave your children, your grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Life to live, let them live. A Jewish life, and to support the Jewish cause. That's my whole life. See, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you agree to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: To let the world know what's going on. We're dead already. How long will I live? Another year. I'm already-- old timer's disease got in my head. So let the people know at least-- because people say, nothing happened, nothing happened, nothing happened, nothing happened. So let the world at least-- I got already two tapes in the schools what I went through. So I think at that time I cried more from here. I was not afraid to work. I was always in business. On the farm, if you want to be a real estate man, you have to know English good. You have to go through the process. And when I came to the Unites States, I didn't go to school here. So to do business, I bought a little farm, I fixed over, I sold it. I make a couple of dollars. And then little by little, another one, another one. And I fixed myself. I had a roof. You now, you have to pay for the roof, $3,000 to fix the roof. So I bought the paper rolls with tar, and I took a guy with me, and I made myself a roof. So I'm not a millionaire, but I made a living. Put two kids through college. I got a house for my daughter. This, I bought a house for. And I helped this daughter too, you know. But you know, this is the way it goes. But to be along, all right, I have a family. Thanks, God. But the real family, at least one sister or at least somebody-- nothing. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think my father maybe helped me to live through. I don't know. Because all this, every night when it was bad times and camp, beating and killing, I had a dream and I saw my father stays with a tallis. And even I said to him, what are you doing here, like this. So I knew right away, I'll have a good day. I will be saved. That's why I go to shul and say kaddish. If it helps, not helps. But if you believe in this, maybe it's good for you. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I lived through. But I made a mistake. I didn't write a book.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for giving us your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you for coming.
